To find the determinant of the given $4 \times 4$ matrix, let's denote the matrix by $A$:
$$
A = \left(\begin{array}{cccc}
 \frac{4}{3} & 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & -\frac{13}{3} \\
 -\frac{13}{3} & \frac{25}{6} & -\frac{19}{6} & \frac{11}{6} \\
 -\frac{23}{6} & \frac{29}{6} & -\frac{10}{3} & -\frac{19}{6} \\
 -\frac{17}{6} & -\frac{7}{6} & \frac{5}{2} & \frac{7}{3}
\end{array}\right)
$$

**Step 1: Simplify the matrix by eliminating fractions.**
The denominators in the matrix are $2$ and $3$. The least common multiple (LCM) of these denominators is $6$.
We can factor out $\frac{1}{6}$ from each row. Since there are $4$ rows, the total factor we pull out is $(\frac{1}{6})^4 = \frac{1}{1296}$.
Let's multiply the entire matrix by $6$ to get an integer matrix $B$:
$$
B = 6A = \left(\begin{array}{cccc}
 8 & 6 & -27 & -26 \\
 -26 & 25 & -19 & 11 \\
 -23 & 29 & -20 & -19 \\
 -17 & -7 & 15 & 14
\end{array}\right)
$$
The relationship between the determinants is $\det(A) = \left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^4 \det(B) = \frac{1}{1296} \det(B)$.
